title: Asthma and chemical bronchitis in vanadium plant workers
abstract:
Asthma and Chemical Bronchitis
in Vanadium Plant Workers
Gernot B. Irsigler, MD, FRCPC,1* Peet J. Visser, MB, ChB,2 and Pedro A.L. Spangenberg
Background Whether vanadium induces bronchial hyperresponsiveness and asthma in
previously normal subjects is unresolved: the two reported series addressing this question
both have shortcomings.
Objective To determine the cause of cough and breathlessness in vanadium plant workers
after variable periods of exposure.
Design Case series of employees presenting with persistent symptoms over a 24-month study
period.
Patients and Methods Forty of an estimated 1,440 patients were investigated by 1) blood
count and serum IgE, 2) intracutaneous allergen skin tests, 3) spirometry, and
4) bronchoprovocation by histamine inhalation or exercise challenge. Exposure was assessed
by measurement of 1) ambient V2O5, NH3 and SO2 over 7 days during the 24-month study
period, 2) urine vanadium concentration at time of ﬁrst presentation.
Results Twelve of 40 subjects had bronchial hyperreactivity (BHR), and these were compared
to 12 age-matched companion subjects whose BHR was normal. In 10, BHR was diagnosed by
histamine inhalation (PC20 0.25–1.82 mg/ml, nl . 8.0 mg/ml), and in six of these the
abnormality was severe (PC20 , 0.5 mg/ml). A further two had BHR by exercise challenge
(FEV1, 600 ml/30% and 770 ml/18% pre/post exercise). After removal from exposure, 9 of the
12 subjects returned for follow-up 5 to 23 months later. BHR was worse in one, still present
although less severe in ﬁve, and was no longer found in one subject. Baseline spirometry
measurements were normal in seven subjects and only mildly impaired in the remaining ﬁve of
the 12 subjects with BHR.
Conclusion This study provides strong supporting evidence that inhaled V205 induces BHR
and asthma in subjects previously free of lung disease; the abnormality may persist for up to
23 months following exposure; routine spirometry will not detect affected subjects. Am. J. Ind.
Med. 35:366–374, 1999.
r 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
KEY WORDS: vanadium; bronchial hyperreactivity; irritant-induced asthma; chemical
bronchitis
INTRODUCTION
Vanadium, because it gives iron tensile strength and
ﬂexibility, is in great demand for the manufacture of hard
steel alloys. In this process, the pentoxide (V2O5) is gener-
ally used. It also ﬁnds application as a coloring agent in the
manufacture of glass, ceramics, and inks and is used in the
production of ﬂuorescent lights, batteries, and color phospho-
rus. Industrial exposure occurs in the course of mining the
ore, the various manufacturing processes listed above, and
also in the process of repairing oil-using boilers, as many
oils contain vanadium.
The route of industrial exposure is inhalation of vana-
dium dust or fumes, particularly the pentoxide form. It is
poorly absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract and not at all
1Pulmonary Unit, Department of Medicine, University of Pretoria, Republic of
South Africa.
2Medical Division, Vanadium Technologies, Steelpoort.
*Correspondence to: Dr. Gernot B. Irsigler, 807 Park Street, Arcadia, Pretoria
0083, South Africa.
Accepted 3 November 1998.
AMERICAN JOURNAL OF INDUSTRIAL MEDICINE 35:366–374 (1999)
r 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
 through the skin, although local contact irritation does occur.
Unlike ammonia or sulphur dioxide, vanadium is odorless
and not readily detectable in ambient air. South Africa is a
large producer of vanadium and, together with China,
Russia, and some Scandinavian countries, accounts for more
than 80% of total world production [Wates et al., 1996].
Symptoms of vanadium toxicity were ﬁrst described by
Dutton [1911] and later by Sjoeberg [1950], Williams
[1952], Kiviluoto et al. [1979] and others. They comprise
conjunctivitis and irritation of the upper respiratory tract:
rhinitis, pharyngitis, and tracheobronchitis. Exposure, even
when prolonged, has not been shown to cause chronic
airways disease, and vanadium is not carcinogenic.
There have been only two reports addressing the
question of vanadium-induced bronchial hyperresponsive-
ness and asthma, and both have shortcomings [Musk and
Tees, 1982; Pistelli et al., 1991]. There are no reports in the
literature of longer term follow-up of affected individuals.
We describe here our observations in 24 vanadium plant
workers presenting with cough and breathing difficulty after
variable periods of exposure. Our major ﬁnding is that
vanadium induces bronchial hyperresponsiveness and asthma
in some previously well individuals and that abnormal
bronchial reactivity may persist for up to 23 months
following removal from exposure.
METHODS
Work Environment
The plant is situated in the Steelpoort District of
Mpumalanga (formerly Eastern Transvaal), South Africa at
an altitude of 850 meters, 300 km by road from Pretoria. It is
located at the foot of two hills, one of which is being mined
for titaniferous magnetite. The opencast mine delivers the
ore to the plant and, through a process of closed circuit
crushing and screening, all material is reduced from 100 mm
feed size to less than 13 mm. After magnetic separation, the
material is milled down to 0.075 mm and then roasted with
soda ash and sodium sulphate in a kiln to form callein,
containing water soluble Na3 VO3, which is inhalable in
vapor form. In this process, carbon dioxide (C02) and
sulphur dioxide (S02) are formed and, after scrubbing, are
released through a stack located some 100 m from the kiln.
Through a process of leaching and precipitation, which
involves the addition of ammonium sulphate and sulphuric
acid, solid ammonium polivanadate (NH4 VO3) powder is
formed. This powder is heated to 600°C, NH3 and S02 gas
being expelled in the process. The material is allowed to cool
and the ﬂake-like ﬁnished product, vanadium pentoxide
(V205), is packed into drums or bags for export, or
converted to ferro-vanadium (FeV) in a separate plant.
Workers are exposed to titaniferous magnetite at the
mill; water soluble Na V03 droplets/solution at the leach;
NH4 V03 dust, V205 fumes, and dust and free NH3 at the
precipitation and fusion sections; and V205, S02, and NH3 at
the pollution control sections of the plant.
The company does regular ﬁxed-point gravimetic sam-
pling as required by the government mining engineer. In
addition, all workers in high-risk areas are supplied with
half-mask respirators with replaceable ﬁlters which are worn
for the duration of the shift. At random intervals during the
year, the entire plant area is monitored by an independent
ﬁrm of consultants, specializing in industrial hygiene, for
S02, NH3, and V205. Despite these measures, our results
showed overexposure in some production workers as evi-
denced by individual urine vanadium levels.
Employee Recruitment
The plant employs some 375 workers, the large major-
ity of whom are recruited from the surrounding rural area
where the main activity is agriculture. Some of the more
specialized workers (engineers, electricians, ﬁtters, and
turners) are recruited from major centers elsewhere. Prospec-
tive employees are seen by an attending physician, complete
a medical questionnaire, and then receive a physical exami-
nation, screening spirometry, chest radiograph, audiometry,
intracutaneous allergy tests for common South African
allergens, and determination of serum IgE as a baseline test.
Workers with current symptoms or previous history of
asthma are not deployed in risk areas of the plant. Medical
examination is repeated annually or sooner, if symptoms
warrant.
Study Plan
This is a case series derived from employees presenting
voluntarily at the plant medical station. The subjects were
selected over a period of 24 months, from October 1995.
Workers presenting with cough, breathing difficulty, or
wheezing were initially seen at the plant medical station and
treatment was prescribed. This generally comprised inhaled
and oral bronchodilators, expectorants, and, in appropriate
circumstances, a short course of corticosteroids. A urine
sample for V205 determination was taken at this time and the
worker was relocated to a low-risk area in the plant. Patients
returning with persistent symptoms, or recurrence of symp-
toms after a period of quiescence, were referred to one of us
(G.B.I.) for assessment in Pretoria. This more detailed
assessment comprised a history and full clinical examination
and the following investigations: 1) blood count and repeat
measurement of serum IgE, 2) repeat intracutaneous aller-
gen skin testing, 3) standard chest X-ray, 4) lung function,
and 5) bronchoprovocation tests. Symptomatic treatment
was continued and the worker returned to work in a low-risk
area outside the plant for variable periods of time, or was
assisted in ﬁnding employment elsewhere. Subjects with
367
Asthma, Bronchitis, and Vanadium

This is a case series derived from employees presenting
voluntarily at the plant medical station. The subjects were
selected over a period of 24 months, from October 1995.
Workers presenting with cough, breathing difficulty, or
wheezing were initially seen at the plant medical station and
treatment was prescribed. This generally comprised inhaled
and oral bronchodilators, expectorants, and, in appropriate
circumstances, a short course of corticosteroids. A urine
sample for V205 determination was taken at this time and the
worker was relocated to a low-risk area in the plant. Patients
returning with persistent symptoms, or recurrence of symp-
toms after a period of quiescence, were referred to one of us
(G.B.I.) for assessment in Pretoria. This more detailed
assessment comprised a history and full clinical examination
and the following investigations: 1) blood count and repeat
measurement of serum IgE, 2) repeat intracutaneous aller-
gen skin testing, 3) standard chest X-ray, 4) lung function,
and 5) bronchoprovocation tests. Symptomatic treatment
was continued and the worker returned to work in a low-risk
area outside the plant for variable periods of time, or was
assisted in ﬁnding employment elsewhere. Subjects with
Asthma, Bronchitis, and Vanadium
asthma were referred to the Government Compensation
Authority for disability assessment.
Intracutaneous allergen skin tests, including histamine
as positive and diluent as negative control, were performed
for 12 common SouthAfrican allergens (Miles Inc., Pharma-
ceutical Division, Elkhart, IN, USA). A positive test was a 3
mm diameter wheal greater than negative control, to two or
more allergens. Total serum IgE (IU/L) was measured by
two-site immunoradiometric assay, utilizing enzyme-labeled
antibody (SanoﬁDiagnostics Pasteur, Inc., Chaska, MN,
An experienced operator performed a maximal expira-
tory/inspiratory ﬂow volume loop according to standard
guidelines [American Thoracic Society, 1995]. The highest
FVC and FEV1 was selected from the best of three consecu-
tive efforts from any acceptable curve. Spirometric measure-
ments were made with a Pneumotach ﬂow sensing device
calibrated to ATS standards on the day of the study (Master
Lab, Erich Jaeger, Wuerzburg, Version 4.0). Inhaled beta-2
adrenergic agents had been discontinued at least 8 hr before
the test in all cases. Obstructive impairment is present if the
FEV1 is less than 80% of predicted and FEV1/FVC ratio is
less than 75%. The pattern of small airways disease is
deﬁned by MMF ,70% in the presence of normal FEV1
[Fishman, 1988]. Predicted values for FVC and FEV1 were
those of the European Community for Coal and Steel
[Quanjer, 1983] corrected by 0.87 for subjects of African
ethnic heritage [Gardner et al., 1989].
The histamine inhalation test was done by the tidal
breathing method of Cockroft et al. [1977]. After three
recordings of baseline FEV1 the subject inhaled nebulized
isotonic saline solution through a nebulizer (Puritan Bennett,
Carlsbad, CA, USA) with an output of 0.13 ml/min, via a
loosely ﬁtting face mask, nose occluded, for 2 min tidal
breathing. FEV1 was recorded at 30 and 90 sec after the
inhalation and the lowest value was taken as baseline for
calculation. Following this, histamine inhalation was done
in a similar way, with the nebulizer ﬁlled with 3 ml
histamine acid phosphate in doubling concentrations (0.125
to 8.0 mg/ml) every 5 min until a fall of 20% or greater in
FEV1 was recorded. The histamine PC20 was calculated by
a linear dose vs. response formula [Cockroft et al., 1983].
Using this technique, the normal response was taken as
PC20 . 8.0 mg/ml [Cockroft et al., 1977]. In one subject
(No. 7), the follow-up test was done with metacholine,
owing to temporary unavailability of histamine phosphate in
South Africa at the time. Concentrations of test substance
and testing protocol were identical to the histamine test.
Exercise challenge was done by free tread mill running
under ambient indoor conditions for 6 min. A positive test
was 200 ml or 15% or greater fall in FEV1 from baseline
[McFadden and Gilbert, 1994]. A positive exercise test very
speciﬁcally establishes the diagnosis of bronchial hyperre-
sponsiveness; it is, however, less sensitive than pharmaco-
logic challenge in detecting borderline cases [Eliasson et al.,
Fixed point gravimetric sampling for measurement of
ambient air V205, S02 and NH3 was carried out by a
government-approved inspection authority over 7 days dur-
ing the period of our investigation.
Urine samples were collected when workers had show-
ered and changed clothes. The vanadium in the urine
specimens was chelated with cupferron and extracted into
IBMK. Analysis was carried out using atomic absorbtion
spectrophotometry on a Perkin Elmer 5000 instrument
[Paschal and Bailey, 1990]. Urine vanadium levels are
reported as mg/g creatinine from which a biologic exposure
index (BEI) was obtained. Reference values for BEI are ,10
µg/g—acceptable range; 10–50 µgm/g—moderate risk;
.50 µg/g—high-risk exposure [Baselt, 1980].
The patient proﬁle of workers consulting the plant
medical station over the last 6 months of our 24-month study
period is given in Table I. Three hundred and sixty (33%) of
the total number of 1,083 patients seen at the clinic
presented with respiratory related complaints: 1,440 patients
over the 24-month study period. Forty were referred for
more detailed assessment because of persistence of symp-
toms, and of these, 12 were found to have bronchial
hyperresponsiveness to inhaled histamine or exercise chal-
lenge, i.e., an estimated incidence of ,1.0%. Of the
remaining 28 patients, 12 were selected as companion
subjects matched for age and smoking habit to eliminate
these factors as causes of bronchial hyperresponsiveness.All
subjects were males, ranging in age from 19 to 60 years, the
majority in their 30s and 40s. A minority were smokers and
none smoked more than 15 cigarettes daily (Tables III
The data of ﬁxed point gravimetric sampling are shown
in Table II. For V205, the recommended limit was exceeded
only in the mills and precipitation/fusion section of the plant.
For S02 and NH3, the kiln section was an area of concern,
and measures have since been instituted to rectify this.
Urine V205 was detectably elevated in 10 of the 12
subjects with abnormal bronchoprovocation tests in whom
this measurement was made, and the BEI was in the toxic
range (.50 µg/g) in three (Subjects 1, 6, and 7; Table III).
Subject 1, a recent employee with the highest recorded level
(180 µg/g) developed cough and breathing difficulty after
only his third 8-hr working shift, having discarded his
protective gear because of the heat. Subjects with normal
bronchoprovocation tests all had elevated urine V205; in only
one did the BEI exceed 50 µg/g (Table IV). There are no
vanadium levels in two of the exposed workers (Table III).
Subject 3, a maintenance worker traversing all areas but
frequently also the precipitation and fusion sections, was
referred from another plant. He had experienced cough and
breathing difficulty of increasing severity over the preceding
9 months. The urine sample of Subject 4 was lost.
It must be emphasized that urine vanadium levels were
determined on spot samples at the time of ﬁrst presentation;
these should be regarded as an index of exposure and should
not be seen as long-term averages.
In seven of the hyperresponsiveness subjects, symp-
toms of cough and breathing difficulty developed within 6
months of commencing work at the plant. In the remaining
ﬁve, the latency period was longer. Of the nonreactors, only
two experienced symptoms within the ﬁrst 6 months.
Six of the 12 subjects exhibiting bronchial hyperreactiv-
ity were employed at the fusion-precipitation section of the
plant, the area of highest ambient V205 (Table II). Three
others rotated through all sections, including the fusion-
precipitation section, in the course of their duties. Of the 12
nonreactive subjects, ﬁve were from the fusion-precipitation
section, the others from different areas in the plant.
The range of serum IgE for reactors was 24–5,225, with
a geometric mean of 952 IU/ml. For nonreactors, correspond-
ing values were 7–11,200, mean 1,771 IU/ml. Differences in
serum IgE between these two groups of subjects were not
signiﬁcant. The generally higher serum levels in this rural
African population (compared to Western peoples) is in
keeping with previous observations [Orren et al., 1975]. Two
and three in each of the two groups of 12 subjects,
respectively, were atopic by intracutaneous allergen testing.
Data for the two groups of subjects are given in Tables
III and IV. Of the 12 subjects with bronchial hyperreactivity,
in 10 this was detected by histamine inhalation, in a further
two by exercise challenge. The range of abnormal response
was 0.25 to 1.82 mg/ml inhaled histamine (normal .8.0
mg/ml) and in 6 of the 10 subjects the abnormality was
severe, i.e., ,0.5 mg/ml. Baseline spirometry measurements
TABLE I. Patient Numbers by Diagnostic Category Consulting the
Vanadium Plant Medical Station Over 6 Months (April to September
1997), Mpumalanga, South Africa
TABLE II. Soluble Vanadium Content, Levels of SO2 and NH3 Measured
in Selected Sites of the Plant*
*Columbus Stainless—Occupational Health Department, Middelburg 1050.
**TWA OEL-RL: time weighted average. Occupational Exposure Limit—Recommended Limit.
Asthma, Bronchitis, and Vanadium
were normal in the majority and the degree of impairment
was minimal in the remainder: three were mildly obstructed
(FEV1 ,80%), a further two had a pattern of small airways
The postexercise fall in FEV1 was striking in the two
subjects tested in this way: 600 ml/30% and 770 ml/18%
from baseline FEV1 (Subjects 8 and 2, respectively).
Of the 12 subjects with abnormal bronchoprovocation
tests, nine returned for follow-up examination 5 to 23
months later, having been relocated either to a low-risk area
outside the plant (general maintenance, gardening, etc.) or
having left the industry altogether. Bronchial reactivity was
greater in one, was still present although at higher threshold
in ﬁve, and could no longer be demonstrated in one (Fig. 1).
In two workers, bronchial hyperresponsiveness was shown
by exercise testing at the ﬁrst examination and by histamine
inhalation at follow-up examination 5 and 14 months later
Of the 12 age-matched companion subjects with normal
provocation tests, 10 were challenged by histamine inhala-
tion and two by exercise testing. Seven individuals in this
group had normal spirometric measurements, two were
TABLE III. Demographic and Laboratory Data in 12 Vanadium Plant Workers With Abnormal Reactivity to Histamine or Exercise Challenge,
*Urine vanadium mg/g creatinine.
**PC20 (1) and (2): histamine/exercise challenge at initial and follow-up visit, respectively.
***WNL 5 within normal limits.
TABLE IV. Demographic and Laboratory Data in 12 Vanadium Plant Workers with Normal Reactivity to Histamine or Exercise Challenge,
FIGURE 1. Individual responses to
histamine inhalation in 12 subjects. The
percent fall in FEV1 from baseline is
plotted against the corresponding dose
of inhaled histamine. Abnormal re-
sponse ,8.0 mg/ml, shaded grey.
mildly obstructed, two had small airways disease pattern,
and one was mildly restricted (VC 78% of normal).
Abnormal lung function was not consistently related to
smoking status in either of the two groups.
This study provides evidence that inhalational exposure
to vanadium induces bronchial hyperresponsiveness and
asthma in subjects previously free of lung disease. Although
some had minor lung function impairment at the time of
presentation, the majority of those affected had normal
spirometry prior to bronchoprovocation testing by histamine
or exercise challenge. Onset of symptoms was generally
within the ﬁrst 6 months of commencing work at the plant,
although in some, the latency period was longer.
Follow-up studies in nine subjects after relocation away
from the plant revealed a trend towards improvement with
passage of time. However, persistent bronchial hyperrespon-
siveness was evident for up to 23 months. Other subjects,
taken from the same work environment, had similarly mild
degrees of spirometric impairment but bronchoprovocation
tests were normal. Urine vanadium levels were detectably
increased in all workers in whom it was measured, attesting
to a level of exposure. Our data show that routine spirometry
will not identify affected workers unless this is combined
with exercise or nonspeciﬁc pharmacological challenge.
There have been only two reports in the literature to
date associating asthma with vanadium exposure. Musk and
Tees [1982] described four workers from a vanadium
reﬁnery in Western Australia presenting with upper respira-
tory symptoms and asthma; in one case, 6 weeks after
cessation of exposure. Lung function tests revealed obstruc-
tive impairment in three and hyperresponsiveness to inhaled
histamine (PC20 ,8.0 mg/ml) in two of their patients. This
was the ﬁrst report incriminating vanadium as a cause of
airways hyperresponsiveness and asthma. However, one of
the four subjects was atopic and all were smokers. More
recently, Pistelli et al. [1991] described 11 workers exposed
to V2O5 in the process of removal of boiler ash. Urine
vanadium levels were increased in all. Interpretation of this
study is problematical in that a 15% fall in FEV1 in response
to metacholine inhalation (rather than 20%) was taken as an
endpoint, increasing the likelihood of including normals
[Eliasson et al., 1992]. Furthermore, applying these less
stringent criteria, only 4 of their 11 subjects had PC15 values
,8.0 mg/ml. A recent cross-sectional survey of 333 employ-
ees at a vanadium plant in South Africa revealed a threefold
increase in asthma-like symptoms in current workers com-
pared to a referent population not exposed to vanadium or
other major respiratory irritants [Kielkowsky and Rees,
Alternative Causes of Bronchial
Could the abnormal histamine responses in our subjects
be due to factors other than industrial exposure? Well-known
biological incitors of bronchial hyperreactivity are atopic
asthma [Cockroft et al., 1977], viral upper respiratory
infection [Empey et al., 1976], and cigarette smoke [Gerard
Nonspeciﬁc hyperresponsiveness of the airways is a
cardinal feature of established clinical asthma. The relation-
ship of bronchial hyperreactivity to atopy in individuals who
are not asthmatic is less clear: according to some, atopy is
strongly correlated with airway hyperresponsiveness [refer-
ences in Cockroft, 1990], others have found no such
correlation [Burrows et al., 1989; Eliasson et al., 1992].
Atopy is deﬁned by elevated serum IgE and response to
intracutaneous testing for common aeroallergens. In native
African populations, serum IgE levels are higher than those
of mixed race or European descent [Orren et al., 1975]. The
differences cannot be explained by helminthic or other
parasitic diseases in the former, in whom high IgE levels
may be genetically predetermined [Haus et al., 1988]. In our
subjects, serum IgE levels were high in both the reactors and
in nonreactive controls. Intracutaneous allergy tests were
positive in only 5 of our 24 subjects and these were similarly
distributed between the two groups. Therefore, atopy, al-
though it may have contributed to bronchial hyperreactivity
in some of our subjects, cannot account for its presence in
Transient bronchial hyperreactivity is a feature of viral
upper respiratory tract infection; the abnormality rarely
persists longer than 2 months [Empey et al., 1976]. None of
our subjects described appropriate symptoms nor did we ﬁnd
corroborative clinical evidence (conjunctivitis, pharyngitis).
Cigarette smoke is unlikely to be a factor—there were only
10 subjects who smoked, none very heavily or for long, and
they were equally distributed between the two groups.
Could other chemicals be involved? Our subjects were
exposed not only to V205 but also to other potentially
asthmogenic stimuli in the course of their working shifts. Of
concern in this regard is possible exposure to S02 and NH3.
S02 on contact with mucosal surface moisture is rapidly
hydrated and then oxidized to form sulphurous acid, a strong
respiratory irritant. Following accidental exposure to near-
lethal concentrations of S02, survivors were left with obstruc-
tive lung function impairment and, in some, bronchial
hyperreactivity to histamine 4 years later [Ha¨rko¨nen et al.,
1983]. Similarly, exposure to overwhelming concentrations
of NH3 fumes induced inﬂammatory airways damage [Wal-
ton, 1973] and even bronchiectasis [Kass et al., 1972] in
survivors; reversible bronchospasm lasting up to 6 months
has been reported in one case report [Flury et al., 1983].
More recently, irritant-induced asthma was described in
three pulp-mill workers exposed repeatedly to less severe
concentrations of a mixture of gases, including SO2 [Chan-
Yeung et al., 1994]. However, in this latter study bronchial
reactivity to metacholine was normal in one and only mildly
At the Steelpoort plant, levels of SO2 and NH3 were
found to periodically exceed the National Institute of
Occupational Safety and Health recommended safety limit
of 2 to 5 ppm and 25 ppm for these two gases, respectively,
but this occurred only infrequently, usually due to scrubbing
problems at the emission stack. None of our subjects had
exposure at the toxic levels described in the above-cited
reports. Furthermore, three of our hyperreactive subjects
were employed at sites where SO2 and NH3 concentrations
are negligible, i.e., mills, belt feeder, and dispatch section of
the plant. It seems unlikely, therefore, that exposure to SO2
and NH3 can account for the persistent bronchial hyperreac-
Pathogenesis and Classification
The pathogenesis underlying bronchial hyperresponsive-
ness in occupational and, indeed, allergic asthma is as yet
incompletely understood. In particular, the mechanisms that
link the presumed inﬂammatory process in the airways to
hyperreactivity is currently under intense investigation and
this subject has recently been reviewed [Haley and Drazen,
Occupational asthma is classiﬁed on clinical grounds
into one of two types, depending on the presence or absence
of latency between exposure and onset of symptoms [Chan-
Yeung and Malo, 1995]. Asthma with latency comprises all
instances where immune (IgE) mechanisms are thought to
be involved. Asthma without latency follows exposure to
irritant gases, fumes, or chemicals. The pathogenesis is
unknown: whereas the pathology in the airways of presumed
irritant asthma is similar to that of the allergic type, recent
studies have shown differences that suggest mechanisms
other than immune processes may be involved [Gautrin et
al., 1994; Chan Yeung et al., 1994]. Asthma without latency
can present as the reactive airways dysfunction syndrome
(RADS) initiated by a single massive exposure to irritant
[Brooks et al., 1985]. This entity appears to be well
established [Kern and Sherman, 1994; Alberts and Brooks,
1996]. The role of repeated low dose exposure to irritant
gases or vapors, on the other hand, is more controversial
[Kennedy, 1992; Kern and Sherman, 1994]. In a widely cited
review, Kennedy [1992] pointed out that no published
articles to date have provided measurement of airways
responsiveness prior to exposure to the offending agent, nor
have the intensities of exposure been quantiﬁed.
The mechanism of asthma induced by vanadium, a
presumed irritant, is likewise unknown. It is tempting to
speculate that the strikingly high levels of serum IgE in some
of our subjects may reﬂect immune mechanisms being
involved, analogous to platinum-induced asthma [Pepys et
al., 1972]. Clearly, further studies are needed to elucidate
this. Also, it is unclear why some workers develop airways
hyperresponsiveness following exposure and others do not.
However, the relation of worker location to symptom onset
suggests that this may be an exposure-intensity effect.
Evidence emerging from this study of overexposure in
some production workers despite protective measures being
in place has led to implementation of structural and organiza-
tional changes at the Steelpoort plant designed to enhance
environmental safety. These measures include installation of
improved air ﬁltration systems; placement of a larger
extractor mechanism at the kiln with scrubber type ﬁltration
prior to disposal into the atmosphere; and relocation of the
dispatch and packaging areas to more distant sites. In
addition, half-mask respirators worn by workers in risk areas
are now inspected, cleaned, and replaced on a daily basis.
In summary, we have shown that inhalation exposure to
vanadium dust or vapors induces airways hyperresponsive-
ness and symptoms of asthma in subjects previously free of
lung disease. Routine spirometry will not detect affected
workers. In other workers presenting with similar symp-
toms, no abnormal bronchial reactivity was found. This
difference may be exposure/dose related. Follow-up studies
after relocation away from the plant in nine subjects reveals
a trend towards abatement, although abnormal bronchial
reactivity could still be demonstrated up to 23 months later.
The mechanisms underlying asthma and bronchial hyperre-
activity induced by vanadium remain to be elucidated.
